topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Precisely characterize how architectural innovations such as mixture-of-experts (MoE) layers alter established scaling laws by training a controlled set of models varying both architecture and scale.

BACKGROUND: Current scaling laws for LLMs have been primarily derived from standard dense Transformer architectures, leaving an open question about how architectural changes—especially sparsely activated MoE models—impact scaling behavior. Understanding whether and how MoE modifies the trade-offs between model size, data, and compute is crucial for predicting returns on investment in these architectures at extreme scales.

APPROACH: Train a systematic grid of models that vary in scale (ranging from 100M to 10B parameters) and architecture (dense Transformer vs. MoE variants with varying numbers of experts). Keep training data and compute budget constant to isolate the effect of architecture on scaling laws. Analyze performance metrics such as perplexity and downstream task accuracy against parameter count and compute to establish updated scaling relationships.

EXPERIMENT: Use a fixed, high-quality web-crawled dataset (e.g., The Pile) and train models on standardized compute budgets, measuring validation cross-entropy and a suite of NLP benchmarks. Quantitatively compare scaling exponents and compute-data-model allocations between dense and MoE models.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats architecture as fixed or only demonstrates MoE gains in isolation, this experiment explicitly disentangles architectural impact on scaling laws through a controlled parametric sweep, enabling predictive scaling models that incorporate architectural innovation rather than relying on dense model extrapolations.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Systematically characterize how architectural modifications alter scaling law parameters by training matched-size LLMs with controlled architecture variants across multiple data scales.

BACKGROUND:  
The impact of architectural changes and training dynamics on the universality and parameterization of scaling laws for LLMs remains poorly understood. Existing scaling laws are largely derived from standard transformer architectures, limiting their applicability to emerging or modified designs. Understanding this relationship is crucial for developing generalized scaling predictions that can guide efficient design of next-generation models.

APPROACH:  
Train a series of large language models with identical parameter counts but varied architectural features (e.g., attention mechanisms, normalization layers, feed-forward block sizes) on matched datasets of increasing scale. Measure the scaling behavior of loss and task performance across model-data size regimes, isolating how specific architectural changes affect scaling exponents and constants. Control training dynamics by keeping hyperparameters stable to ensure fair comparisons.

EXPERIMENT:  
Use publicly available corpora (e.g., The Pile subsets at multiple scales) to train models ranging from 100M to 1B parameters with several architectural variants. Evaluate held-out loss curves and downstream task performance over increasing data volumes to extract and compare scaling laws, analyzing shifts in scaling parameters quantitatively.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which primarily focuses on standard transformer architectures or single modifications, this experiment systematically isolates multiple architectural factors across data scales, providing the first controlled empirical basis for architecture-agnostic scaling laws. This enables principled predictions for novel model designs beyond current empirical regimes.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: A controlled large-scale study to quantify how varying training dataset quality and diversity affects the scaling behavior of LLM performance across multiple compute budgets.

BACKGROUND: The role of data quality and composition in shaping scaling laws remains unclear, limiting our ability to optimize dataset curation for efficient model training. Without understanding how data attributes influence scaling efficiency, practitioners risk over- or under-investing in data collection, impacting both cost and final model quality.

APPROACH: We will construct multiple versions of large-scale training corpora systematically varying in quality (e.g., noise level, factuality) and diversity (e.g., topical breadth, domain variety) while controlling for total dataset size. We will then train a suite of transformer language models across different compute regimes on each dataset variant, measuring performance scaling curves and their deviations.

EXPERIMENT: Using established benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BIG-bench) as evaluation metrics, we will train models at 3 distinct scales (small, medium, large) on datasets with precisely quantified quality/diversity metrics, then analyze how scaling exponents and saturation points shift with data composition.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that often treats data as a monolithic variable, this experiment systematically disentangles and quantifies the impact of granular data attributes on scaling laws, enabling predictive modeling of dataset curation trade-offs for efficient LLM training.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Systematically measure the effect of varying data quality on scaling laws by training a suite of LLMs on datasets with controlled noise and distribution shifts.

BACKGROUND: The impact of data quality and distribution shifts on scaling behavior remains uncharacterized, limiting the applicability of existing scaling laws derived from clean, static datasets to real-world settings. Understanding how data imperfections alter performance gains at scale is crucial to build robust, reliable LLMs that maintain expected improvements in diverse environments.

APPROACH: Construct multiple training corpora derived from a base high-quality dataset by introducing controlled amounts of noise (e.g., label errors, corrupted tokens) and distribution shifts (e.g., domain-specific subsets, temporal splits). Train a range of models varying in parameter count on each dataset variant to measure changes in scaling curves. Analyze how degradation in data quality shifts optimal model size, data efficiency, and test performance scaling.

EXPERIMENT: Use a standardized large-scale language modeling benchmark such as The Pile or C4, generate noisy and shifted variants, and train transformer models ranging from 10M to 10B parameters. Evaluate test loss and downstream task performance across all conditions to quantify deviations from clean data scaling laws.

NOVELTY: Prior scaling law studies have mostly used fixed, clean datasets; this experiment introduces systematic, quantifiable degradations and distribution shifts to directly measure their influence on scaling dynamics, enabling principled adjustments of scaling predictions for real-world, noisy data regimes.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Conduct a controlled scaling experiment varying dataset quality and distribution to quantify their impact on scaling laws and model generalization.

BACKGROUND: The interaction between data quality, distribution shifts, and scaling laws is poorly understood, which prevents reliable prediction of model performance in real-world, non-i.i.d. settings. Understanding this interaction is critical to deploying robust language models that maintain performance when faced with varied and noisy data.

APPROACH: Train a series of transformer-based language models at multiple scales (small, medium, large) on datasets systematically manipulated for quality (e.g., noise injection, filtering) and distribution shifts (e.g., domain changes). Measure scaling behavior across these controlled data conditions to isolate how data properties affect the scaling exponent and generalization gaps.

EXPERIMENT: Use a well-established corpus (e.g., C4 or The Pile) as base data, create variations introducing controlled noise levels and domain shifts, then train models at 3 scale levels with identical compute budgets. Evaluate held-out perplexity and out-of-distribution generalization metrics to quantify deviations from standard scaling law predictions.

NOVELTY: Prior scaling law research assumes fixed, high-quality datasets or does not systematically vary data distribution; this experiment uniquely isolates data quality and shift effects on scaling, directly linking data properties to model scaling behavior in a controlled, empirical manner.

IDEA: Develop a high-throughput experimental platform combining single-molecule FRET and deep learning to map and predict the dynamic conformational ensembles of intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs).

BACKGROUND: Accurately modeling intrinsically disordered regions remains an open challenge because these protein segments do not adopt a stable 3D structure but exist as dynamic ensembles. Current structure prediction methods like AlphaFold provide static snapshots, missing the functionally critical conformational heterogeneity of IDRs that governs their regulatory roles.

APPROACH: We will generate large-scale single-molecule FRET datasets capturing distance distributions within diverse IDRs under physiological conditions, producing experimental ensemble fingerprints. These data will train novel deep learning models designed to infer IDR conformational ensembles from sequence, integrating biophysical principles and sequence context to predict dynamic structural distributions rather than single static structures.

EXPERIMENT: Validate the predictive model on a test set of IDRs with independently measured FRET or NMR ensemble data, quantifying accuracy via metrics such as ensemble distance distributions and conformational entropy. Success is defined by high correlation between predicted and experimentally observed ensemble features.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work relying on static structural models or limited biophysical data, this approach leverages quantitative single-molecule ensemble measurements coupled with deep learning to directly model IDR conformational heterogeneity, enabling functionally relevant predictions unavailable through current methods.

IDEA: Develop a high-throughput experimental platform combining time-resolved cryo-EM and deep learning to map protein conformational ensembles and transitions, enabling accurate prediction of functional dynamics beyond static structures.

BACKGROUND: Current protein structure predictors, including AlphaFold, generate a single static conformation and fail to capture the dynamic conformational landscapes proteins adopt to perform functions such as allosteric regulation and ligand binding. Without detailed knowledge of multiple functional states and transition pathways, computational models cannot accurately predict mechanisms critical for drug design and fundamental biology.

APPROACH: We propose to systematically collect time-resolved cryo-EM datasets of select model proteins under varying ligand and environmental conditions to capture multiple conformational states and transitions. These datasets will be used to train a novel deep learning model that integrates structural snapshots and temporal dynamics to predict conformational ensembles and transition kinetics from sequence and biochemical context.

EXPERIMENT: Select 3-5 well-characterized proteins with known functional dynamics and generate cryo-EM maps at multiple time points following rapid perturbations (e.g., ligand addition). Train and validate the model on these data, measuring predictive accuracy by comparing predicted ensembles and transition rates against experimentally observed states using metrics such as ensemble RMSD distributions and kinetic rate constants.

NOVELTY: This approach uniquely combines time-resolved structural data with machine learning to move beyond static structure prediction, explicitly modeling conformational heterogeneity and dynamics, whereas prior methods rely on static datasets and lack temporal resolution.

IDEA: Use time-resolved cryo-electron microscopy combined with machine learning to capture and model multiple conformational states of a dynamic protein, enabling accurate prediction of its conformational ensemble beyond static structures.

BACKGROUND: Current protein structure prediction methods, including AlphaFold, provide high-accuracy static models but fail to capture the dynamic conformational landscape proteins adopt under physiological conditions. Since protein function often depends on transitions between multiple states, a method that experimentally resolves and computationally models these states is essential to overcome this limitation and enable functional insights and drug design targeting dynamic conformations.

APPROACH: We will select a well-characterized, conformationally dynamic protein (e.g., a molecular chaperone or ion channel) and perform time-resolved cryo-EM under varying physiological conditions to experimentally capture multiple functionally relevant conformations. These structural snapshots will be combined with unsupervised machine learning clustering to identify distinct states, which will then be used to train a predictive model that infers the conformational ensemble from sequence and environmental parameters.

EXPERIMENT: Generate a large cryo-EM dataset of the target protein sampled across different ligand or ionic conditions; apply clustering algorithms to segment distinct conformations; validate the predictive model by comparing predicted ensembles against independent smFRET or NMR dynamic data to assess agreement in conformational heterogeneity and population distributions.

NOVELTY: This approach uniquely integrates high-resolution time-resolved structural data with machine learning to move beyond single static models, explicitly capturing protein conformational dynamics and enabling predictive modeling of multiple functional states—advancing far beyond current static structure prediction paradigms.

IDEA: Develop and validate a high-throughput single-molecule FRET (smFRET) assay combined with machine learning to map protein conformational ensembles and dynamics, enabling prediction of multiple functional states beyond static structures.

BACKGROUND: Current protein structure predictors, including AlphaFold, output static models that fail to capture the dynamic conformational landscapes critical for protein function, such as allosteric transitions and signaling states. Accurately predicting these conformational ensembles remains an open problem, limiting mechanistic understanding and drug design targeting dynamic proteins.

APPROACH: We will design a systematic smFRET screening platform targeting key residues across diverse proteins known to undergo conformational changes, generating experimentally derived distance distributions under varying conditions. These rich dynamic datasets will train machine learning models to predict conformational ensembles from sequence and limited structural input, linking sequence features to dynamic states.

EXPERIMENT: Using a benchmark set of 10 proteins with well-characterized conformational dynamics, we will measure thousands of distance distributions via smFRET and correlate them with existing static structures. Model performance will be assessed by how well predicted ensembles reproduce experimentally observed FRET efficiency distributions and known functional states.

NOVELTY: This approach directly integrates quantitative, residue-level dynamic data into predictive modeling, moving beyond static snapshots; combining high-throughput smFRET with machine learning creates a new paradigm for capturing protein dynamics that is scalable and experimentally grounded, unlike prior purely computational or low-throughput experimental methods.

IDEA: Develop a high-throughput single-molecule FRET (smFRET) assay combined with machine learning to map protein conformational ensembles and dynamics, enabling accurate prediction of dynamic structural states beyond static models.

BACKGROUND: Current computational methods such as AlphaFold predict static protein structures but fail to capture the full spectrum of biologically relevant conformations and dynamic transitions critical for understanding allostery, ligand binding, and function. Experimental data on conformational ensembles remain sparse and low-throughput, limiting training and validation of dynamic prediction models.

APPROACH: We will design a smFRET platform to systematically probe multiple labeled sites on diverse proteins under varying conditions, generating rich datasets of distance distributions reflecting conformational ensembles. These data will be integrated with advanced machine learning algorithms to train predictive models that link sequence and environment to dynamic structural landscapes.

EXPERIMENT: Test the approach on a benchmark set of proteins with known dynamic behavior by measuring smFRET distance distributions across labeled variants and comparing predicted conformational ensembles to the experimental data using metrics such as ensemble similarity and kinetic rate constants.

NOVELTY: This approach uniquely combines high-throughput single-molecule biophysics with machine learning to directly quantify and predict protein dynamics at scale, overcoming the static limitation of current methods like AlphaFold and enabling accurate modeling of functionally relevant conformational heterogeneity.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Demonstrate high-fidelity, fault-tolerant logical qubit initialization and readout in a NISQ device by experimentally characterizing and mitigating correlated and non-Markovian noise effects during these processes.

BACKGROUND: Fault-tolerant initialization and readout of logical qubits remain unresolved challenges in NISQ devices due to complex noise sources, including correlations and memory effects, that cause initialization and measurement errors to propagate and degrade logical qubit performance. Without reliable logical state preparation and measurement under realistic noise, scalable quantum error correction cannot be practically realized.

APPROACH: We propose to experimentally implement and benchmark multiple initialization and readout protocols on a superconducting or trapped-ion quantum processor, incorporating noise spectroscopy techniques to characterize correlated and non-Markovian noise during these operations. Based on this noise characterization, we will design tailored pulse sequences and error detection schemes to suppress error propagation in logical qubit preparation and measurement.

EXPERIMENT: Perform repeated logical qubit initialization and readout cycles using a small QEC code (e.g., the [[5,1,3]] code or a surface code patch), measure logical state fidelity and error rates under varying noise conditions, and compare baseline and noise-tailored protocols. Metrics include logical error rates, initialization and readout fidelity, and temporal noise correlation functions obtained from qubit tomography.

NOVELTY: This experiment directly targets realistic correlated and non-Markovian noise effects during logical initialization and readout, moving beyond idealized noise assumptions prevalent in prior work, and integrates noise-tailored control methods with live benchmarking on NISQ devices, enabling practical fault-tolerance advances in near-term quantum hardware.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Demonstrate a real-time adaptive decoding protocol on a small-scale NISQ device that significantly reduces logical error rates under realistic noise within the device’s temporal and computational constraints.

BACKGROUND: Real-time adaptive decoding algorithms that can process error syndromes efficiently on NISQ hardware remain elusive due to limited classical processing speed and tight latency requirements. Without timely decoding, errors may propagate unchecked, severely limiting the effectiveness of QEC on near-term devices. Developing a fast, hardware-compatible decoder is critical to enabling practical error correction.

APPROACH: We propose to implement a lightweight, hardware-optimized neural network decoder trained on device-specific noise data, deployed on a classical co-processor integrated with a superconducting qubit platform. The decoder will process syndrome measurements in real-time during repeated rounds of a small surface code, adapting dynamically to drift and correlated errors. Latency and resource usage will be minimized by pruning and quantizing the neural network architecture.

EXPERIMENT: Execute multiple rounds of surface code error correction on a 5-to-9 qubit superconducting device while running the hardware-accelerated decoder concurrently. Measure logical error rates and decoding latency under varying noise levels and compare against standard offline decoding benchmarks to quantify performance gains.

NOVELTY: This experiment uniquely integrates a tailored, resource-efficient neural decoder with real-time syndrome extraction on NISQ hardware, bridging the gap between theoretical decoding algorithms and practical, low-latency error correction. Prior work has not demonstrated adaptive decoding within the strict timing and computational constraints of existing NISQ devices.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Demonstrate a tailored quantum error correcting code optimized for device-specific noise on a NISQ processor to significantly reduce logical error rates compared to standard codes.

BACKGROUND: Current QEC codes are designed under idealized noise assumptions that poorly match the complex, correlated, and non-Markovian error patterns observed in NISQ devices, leading to limited practical error suppression. Efficiently correcting these realistic noise profiles remains an open challenge that directly impacts the feasibility of near-term error mitigation and logical qubit stabilization.

APPROACH: Characterize the full noise spectrum and correlations of a target NISQ device via gate set tomography and noise spectroscopy. Use this empirical noise model to design a customized QEC code or decoding strategy—such as an adapted surface code variant or tailored stabilizer set—optimized through numerical simulations. Implement this code on the device and compare logical error rates against baseline standard codes under identical conditions.

EXPERIMENT: Perform repeated rounds of syndrome extraction and error correction with both the tailored and standard codes, measuring logical qubit fidelity and error rates over circuit depth. Quantify improvement as a statistically significant reduction in logical error probability and extended logical qubit lifetime.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work relying on generic noise models, this experiment directly incorporates experimentally measured, device-specific noise characteristics into the QEC code design and validation, enabling a practical demonstration of improved error correction performance tailored to real NISQ hardware constraints.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop and experimentally validate a real-time, hardware-aware decoding algorithm with ultra-low latency on a superconducting NISQ device, demonstrating practical fault-tolerant error correction under realistic constraints.

BACKGROUND: Efficient decoding algorithms compatible with limited classical processing power and strict latency constraints remain an unsolved challenge for NISQ devices. Current decoders either require excessive classical resources or introduce delays that prevent timely error correction, resulting in prohibitively high logical error rates and impeding scalability.

APPROACH: We propose to design a hardware-tailored lightweight decoder based on a neural-network-assisted look-up table combined with streaming syndrome data processing integrated directly with the quantum control electronics. This approach exploits device-specific noise characteristics and connectivity to minimize computational overhead and enable sub-microsecond syndrome decoding.

EXPERIMENT: Implement the decoder on a commercial FPGA tightly coupled with a superconducting quantum processor running a small surface code; measure the decoder latency, logical error rates under repeated error correction cycles, and compare against baseline software decoders operating off-line.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior software-based or offline decoders, this work experimentally integrates and benchmarks a co-designed decoding algorithm embedded within hardware control loops, providing the first demonstration of real-time quantum error correction decoding within NISQ hardware constraints.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Demonstrate real-time low-latency decoding of correlated, non-Pauli errors on a mid-scale NISQ device using a neural-network-based decoder integrated with in-hardware feedback control.

BACKGROUND: Efficient and scalable decoding algorithms compatible with NISQ constraints remain an open challenge, as existing decoders either assume simplified noise models or require excessive classical resources, limiting timely syndrome processing and error correction. This bottleneck hinders practical implementation of QEC protocols that can keep pace with the device’s coherence times and error rates.

APPROACH: We propose to develop and deploy a neural-network decoder trained on experimental syndrome data that captures realistic correlated, non-Pauli noise patterns from a NISQ device. The decoder will be implemented on a low-latency FPGA or similar classical co-processor tightly integrated with the quantum hardware to enable real-time syndrome decoding and immediate feedback-based correction.

EXPERIMENT: Using a superconducting or trapped-ion device with ~20–50 qubits running a small surface or subsystem code, collect syndrome measurements under natural noise conditions to train and validate the decoder. Measure logical error rates and latency of the decoding and feedback loop, comparing with standard lookup-table or belief propagation decoders.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior decoders trained on idealized noise models and run offline, this approach leverages real-time hardware-in-the-loop decoding of experimentally characterized correlated noise, pushing the boundary on practical, scalable QEC decoding in NISQ-era devices.

IDEA: Develop a controlled synthetic data generation framework with ground-truth latent confounders to benchmark and improve causal discovery algorithms’ identifiability under latent confounding.

BACKGROUND: Identifiability under latent confounding remains a fundamental open problem because standard causal discovery methods assume causal sufficiency, which is rarely true in practice. Without reliably addressing hidden confounders, inferred causal graphs can be severely biased, undermining their utility in critical decision-making domains such as healthcare or economics.

APPROACH: We propose to construct a comprehensive suite of synthetic datasets generated from structural causal models explicitly incorporating latent confounders with varying strengths and complexities. These datasets will serve as a benchmark to systematically evaluate existing and novel causal discovery algorithms designed to detect or adjust for hidden confounding, leveraging techniques such as instrumental variables, proxy variables, and latent variable graphical models.

EXPERIMENT: We will measure algorithm performance by comparing recovered causal graphs to the known ground-truth using metrics like structural hamming distance, edge orientation accuracy, and false discovery rate across multiple latent confounding regimes. This evaluation will identify specific settings where identifiability is achievable and guide development of improved methods.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work relying on real-world datasets with unknown confounding or simplistic simulations, our framework precisely controls latent confounding factors and provides a standardized, reproducible benchmark specifically targeting the fundamental identifiability challenge, enabling decisive empirical insights and methodological advances.

IDEA: Leverage a large-scale, nonlinear, non-Gaussian synthetic benchmark with known ground-truth to rigorously test and validate new causal direction inference methods that exploit nonlinear asymmetries in the data.

BACKGROUND: Distinguishing cause from effect in nonlinear, non-Gaussian models remains an open problem because standard linear-Gaussian assumptions fail to capture complex real-world dynamics, making causal direction ambiguous. Without definitive benchmarks and validation, progress is blocked by uncertainty about method reliability and generalizability.

APPROACH: Construct a comprehensive synthetic dataset generator that simulates diverse nonlinear causal mechanisms with a broad range of noise distributions beyond Gaussianity, including heavy-tailed and multimodal noise. Develop and benchmark causal discovery algorithms that explicitly model nonlinearities and non-Gaussian noise, using asymmetry-based criteria to infer causal direction.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate competing causal direction methods on this synthetic benchmark by measuring accuracy in recovering known causal directions, robustness to noise type and strength, and computational scalability. Quantitatively compare performance using metrics such as the area under the ROC curve (AUC) and structural Hamming distance.

NOVELTY: This approach combines principled synthetic data generation with a systematic evaluation framework tailored to nonlinear, non-Gaussian scenarios, providing the first rigorous experimental platform to decisively validate causal direction inference methods beyond standard assumptions.

IDEA: Leveraging synthetic interventions on simulated nonlinear, non-Gaussian systems to develop and validate a novel causal direction inference method that generalizes without parametric assumptions.

BACKGROUND: Determining causal direction in nonlinear, non-Gaussian settings remains a fundamental open problem because existing methods either rely on restrictive assumptions or lack theoretical guarantees. Real-world causal mechanisms often involve complex nonlinearities and unknown noise distributions, limiting the applicability of current approaches. A well-designed experiment using controlled synthetic data can decisively reveal whether new algorithmic principles can reliably identify causal direction under these challenging conditions.

APPROACH: We propose constructing a large suite of simulated causal models with diverse nonlinear structural equations and complex, unknown noise processes to comprehensively test and train a causal direction inference algorithm based on information-theoretic criteria and conditional independence patterns. The method will avoid parametric assumptions by using flexible nonparametric estimators and will incorporate recent advances in kernel-based or deep-learning-based dependence measures.

EXPERIMENT: We will generate thousands of synthetic datasets with known ground-truth causal directions and systematically vary nonlinear functional forms and noise types. Performance will be measured by accuracy in correctly recovering causal direction, robustness to noise, and ability to generalize to unseen functional classes. Comparisons to state-of-the-art methods on benchmark datasets will validate improvements.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either assumes additive noise models or simple noise distributions, this experiment explicitly targets complex, realistic nonlinear and non-Gaussian scenarios using large-scale synthetic interventions to rigorously test causal direction methods without parametric constraints, potentially establishing a new benchmark for reliable causal inference in complex systems.

IDEA: Leveraging targeted, minimal interventions in a synthetic high-dimensional system to break causal equivalence classes and enable unique causal structure identification from predominantly observational data.

BACKGROUND: A key unsolved problem in causal discovery is the fundamental limitation of purely observational data to identify unique causal graphs, especially in complex systems where multiple causal models explain the data equally well. Without interventional signals, causal discovery methods often return only equivalence classes rather than definitive causal directions, restricting practical utility.

APPROACH: We propose designing a synthetic high-dimensional system with known nonlinear causal mechanisms and hidden confounders, then applying a small number of carefully chosen, minimal interventions on selected variables. These interventions, combined with observational data, will be used to test whether they enable algorithms to reliably distinguish between competing causal structures and recover the true causal graph uniquely.

EXPERIMENT: We will generate synthetic datasets with known ground truth graphs and simulate observational data alongside a handful of interventions targeting variables chosen via an information-theoretic criterion. Performance will be measured by the accuracy of recovered causal graphs compared to ground truth, quantified using structural Hamming distance and edge orientation precision.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focusing on either purely observational or fully interventional settings, this approach systematically investigates the minimal intervention regime in high-dimensional nonlinear systems, aiming to characterize how few and which interventions suffice to break equivalence classes and improve identifiability in causal discovery.

IDEA: Develop and validate a benchmark experimental platform combining synthetic and semi-synthetic datasets with controlled latent confounding to rigorously evaluate and improve identifiability of causal structures under latent confounding.

BACKGROUND: Identifiability under latent confounding remains a critical unsolved problem because unmeasured confounders induce spurious associations that current causal discovery methods cannot reliably disentangle without strong, often unverifiable assumptions. This gap severely limits causal claims in settings where latent variables are common, such as epidemiology and social science, and no standardized framework exists to systematically test and improve methods under realistic confounding scenarios.

APPROACH: We propose creating a suite of benchmark datasets that embed known causal graphs with explicitly controlled latent confounders, leveraging both fully synthetic data and semi-synthetic data generated by injecting latent confounding into real-world observational datasets. We will then apply existing causal discovery algorithms and novel latent confounding adjustment methods to this benchmark, quantifying identifiability improvements via precision, recall, and structural Hamming distance metrics against ground truth.

EXPERIMENT: To test this platform, we will generate multiple datasets varying in confounding strength, sample size, and graph complexity, then evaluate and compare causal discovery algorithms’ ability to identify true causal edges while controlling false positives due to latent confounding, using standard benchmarks and proposed metrics.

NOVELTY: This approach differs from prior work by providing a unified, experimentally controlled testbed explicitly designed to isolate and measure the impact of latent confounding on causal identifiability, enabling principled, reproducible advances rather than relying on unverifiable assumptions or purely theoretical guarantees.

IDEA: Develop and validate a nanoscale synaptic device fabrication process that achieves sub-5% device-to-device variability, enabling energy-efficient large-scale neuromorphic networks.

BACKGROUND: Scalable, low-power synaptic device variability control remains a critical barrier to energy-efficient neuromorphic computing because high variability degrades network accuracy and forces energy-expensive calibration or redundancy. Without reliable nanoscale devices exhibiting minimal variability, scaling neuromorphic hardware to practical sizes with low energy consumption is blocked.

APPROACH: We will design and fabricate memristive synaptic devices using an optimized atomic layer deposition (ALD) process combined with in-line process control and novel material doping strategies to reduce stochastic filament formation variability. Device arrays will be characterized electrically to quantify variability metrics under typical neuromorphic operating conditions.

EXPERIMENT: We will fabricate multiple 1k-device arrays and measure cycle-to-cycle and device-to-device conductance variability at synaptic weight ranges relevant for spiking neural networks, comparing standard and optimized fabrication batches. Network-level impact will be assessed by simulating spiking neural networks using measured device models to quantify accuracy and energy efficiency improvements.

NOVELTY: This experiment directly targets variability reduction via process-level innovations rather than algorithmic compensation, providing the first demonstration of sub-5% variability in memristive synapses at scale, a threshold hypothesized to unlock significant energy savings in neuromorphic systems.

IDEA: Develop and experimentally validate a fabrication protocol that minimizes synaptic device variability and drift in large-scale memristor arrays through controlled material deposition and post-fabrication annealing.

BACKGROUND: Scalable, low-power synaptic device variability control remains a critical barrier to reliable neuromorphic computing. Nanoscale synaptic devices such as memristors often exhibit significant cycle-to-cycle and device-to-device variability and drift, which degrade system-level performance and increase energy consumption through repeated error correction or over-provisioning.

APPROACH: We propose a systematic study combining atomic layer deposition (ALD) with controlled post-fabrication thermal annealing to reduce material defects and stabilize device switching behavior in large memristor crossbar arrays. We will characterize variability and drift statistically over thousands of devices and cycles, correlating it with process parameters to identify optimal fabrication conditions.

EXPERIMENT: Fabricate multiple memristor arrays with varying ALD process parameters and annealing temperatures; measure device-to-device and cycle-to-cycle resistance variability and drift under repeated switching using high-throughput electrical characterization; evaluate variability impact on neuromorphic inference accuracy using a standard benchmark dataset (e.g., MNIST) mapped onto the array.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely combines precision materials engineering with large-scale statistical analysis in operational arrays rather than isolated devices, enabling direct translation of reduced device variability into system-level neuromorphic performance improvements without added energy overhead.

IDEA: Demonstrate that adaptive calibration circuits using on-chip stochastic feedback can compensate device-level variability in memristive synapses with under 10% energy overhead.

BACKGROUND: Robust device-level variability compensation remains a critical bottleneck in neuromorphic computing, as inherent stochasticity and parameter drift in nanoscale devices degrade accuracy and increase power consumption when corrected by conventional methods. Efficient compensation without significant energy or area penalty is essential to realizing reliable, energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware.

APPROACH: We propose designing and fabricating a memristor-based synapse array integrated with lightweight on-chip adaptive calibration circuits that use stochastic feedback signals to continuously tune device parameters. This approach exploits device noise characteristics rather than fighting them, enabling real-time compensation with minimal additional circuitry and power.

EXPERIMENT: Fabricate a prototype array and benchmark synaptic weight stability and network inference accuracy under controlled variability conditions using standard neuromorphic benchmarks such as N-MNIST, measuring energy consumption of the calibration circuits versus baseline.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior static or software-based compensation methods, this work integrates adaptive, noise-aware hardware calibration directly on-chip, significantly reducing energy overhead and enabling scalable variability compensation in emerging memristive neuromorphic systems.

IDEA: Demonstrate a scalable, ultra-low-power synaptic plasticity mechanism using a memristor-based array implementing spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) at million-synapse scale.

BACKGROUND: Scalable low-power synaptic plasticity implementation remains an open problem because current biologically plausible plasticity mechanisms either do not scale efficiently or consume excessive energy when implemented in hardware. Without such scalable, energy-efficient plasticity, neuromorphic systems cannot adapt in real time at large scale, limiting their practical utility.

APPROACH: Design and fabricate a memristor crossbar array integrated with CMOS spike generation circuits to implement STDP learning rules locally at each synapse. Use device-level programming and circuit optimizations to minimize power per synaptic update, targeting energy budgets below 10 fJ per event. Validate plasticity behavior across arrays of at least one million synapses.

EXPERIMENT: Measure power consumption, plasticity accuracy (weight update fidelity), and scalability by programming spiking input patterns from neuromorphic benchmark datasets (e.g., N-MNIST) and evaluating learning convergence and energy per synaptic event. Compare results against state-of-the-art digital and analog implementations.

NOVELTY: This experiment uniquely combines large-scale memristive hardware with biologically realistic STDP at ultra-low energy levels, surpassing existing work that is either small scale, non-adaptive, or power-hungry, thereby addressing both scalability and energy efficiency simultaneously.

IDEA: Demonstrate scalable and stable nanoscale synaptic plasticity using a novel low-energy memristive device under real-world operating conditions.

BACKGROUND: Achieving reliable synaptic plasticity at the device level with low energy consumption and long-term stability remains a critical unsolved problem in neuromorphic computing. Current nanoscale devices either consume excessive power or degrade quickly, limiting their practical use for learning and adaptation in energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware.

APPROACH: We will engineer a memristive device based on a novel oxide heterostructure optimized for low switching energy and retention stability. The device will be subjected to repeated potentiation and depression cycles under varying temperature and voltage conditions to emulate synaptic plasticity. Electrical characterization and in situ retention tests will quantify energy consumption, endurance, and stability metrics relevant to neuromorphic operation.

EXPERIMENT: The device will be integrated into a test platform for repeated spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) protocol executions. Key metrics include switching energy per synaptic event, cycle-to-cycle variability, and retention time under accelerated aging conditions. Performance will be benchmarked against existing memristive synapses.

NOVELTY: This experiment uniquely combines a new oxide heterostructure design with rigorous real-world stress testing protocols to simultaneously optimize energy efficiency and synaptic reliability—addressing the scalability and stability bottleneck that previous device demonstrations have not resolved.
